skill.name: busybox-on-windows
skill.description: How to use a Win32 build of BusyBox to run many of the standard UNIX command line tools on Windows.
skill.summary: How to use a Win32 build of BusyBox to run many of the standard UNIX command line tools on Windows.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static findings for PowerShell, cmd.exe, registry access, and hardcoded URLs are mostly true positives as documented manual commands. The main risk is that the skill tells users to download and run a third-party Windows executable, but no malicious intent, prompt injection, credential access, or data exfiltration was found.

medium_findings.title: Third-Party Binary Download and Execution Guidance
medium_findings.description: The skill instructs users to download BusyBox Windows executables from an external site and then run busybox.exe commands. This is a legitimate setup pattern, but it creates supply-chain risk if the binary source is compromised or not independently verified.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The download URLs and subsequent busybox.exe usage are explicit in the skill. The risk is contextual supply-chain exposure, not confirmed malware.

medium_findings.title: Manual PowerShell and Windows Shell Commands
medium_findings.description: The skill provides PowerShell commands and explains how to invoke them through cmd.exe. These commands are part of the stated Windows setup workflow, but users should review them before running them.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The command execution guidance is directly present and semantically relevant. It appears user-initiated and administrative rather than hidden or automated.

low_findings.title: Markdown Backtick Command Examples Dismissed
low_findings.description: The static Ruby or shell backtick findings are Markdown inline code spans in documentation. They show commands for a user to review and run manually, and they are not Ruby execution syntax in an executable script.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The only scanned file is Markdown, and the flagged backticks surround documentation examples. No Ruby file or executable Ruby context exists in the file structure.

low_findings.title: Windows Registry Read for Version Detection
low_findings.description: The skill reads a Windows registry path to print OS version information. This is a read-only diagnostic command and does not show evidence of persistence, privilege escalation, or secret collection.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The registry path and selected properties are visible and limited to Windows version metadata. No write operation or suspicious registry key is present.

low_findings.title: Reference Links to External Documentation
low_findings.description: The skill includes hardcoded external URLs for BusyBox documentation and source references. These links support the described tool but should be treated as external trust dependencies.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs are explicit documentation references. They do not include evidence of exfiltration or tracking parameters.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptographic Algorithm Finding Dismissed
low_findings.description: The static weak cryptographic algorithm finding at the description line appears to be a false positive. No evidence of cryptographic code, hashing, encryption, or algorithm selection was found in the skill.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Line 3 is a natural-language description of a Win32 BusyBox build. It contains no cryptographic operation or weak algorithm usage.

dangerous_patterns.title: External Executable Download
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill documents Invoke-WebRequest commands that save remote executable files as busybox.exe. This pattern can be dangerous when source authenticity is not verified.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The remote executable download pattern is explicit and directly tied to later execution guidance. The file uses HTTPS, which reduces transport risk but does not verify binary integrity.

dangerous_patterns.title: Local Command Execution
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill instructs users to run busybox.exe and use it as a command prefix. This is expected functionality for the skill but remains an external command execution pattern.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The execution guidance is visible and intentional. There is no evidence that commands are hidden, automatically triggered, or constructed from untrusted input.

content.value_statement: Windows users often need familiar Unix commands without installing a full Unix-like environment. This skill explains how to use a Win32 BusyBox binary through PowerShell or cmd.exe.
actual_capabilities: Explains when the skill should be used on Windows systems.
Shows how to identify CPU and Windows version details.
Lists BusyBox download options for x86, x64, Unicode, and ARM builds.
Describes how to run BusyBox commands from PowerShell or cmd.exe.
Points users to BusyBox documentation and the original BusyBox project.
limitations: It does not verify downloaded executable checksums or signatures.
It does not install BusyBox automatically.
It is intended only for Windows environments.
It does not document every BusyBox command or option.

use_cases.title: Add Unix Commands to Windows
use_cases.description: Use BusyBox to run common commands such as ls from a Windows terminal.

use_cases.title: Prepare Lightweight Shell Tooling
use_cases.description: Set up a single executable that provides many Unix-style command line utilities.

use_cases.title: Check Windows Compatibility
use_cases.description: Review CPU and OS version commands before selecting a BusyBox build.

prompt_templates.title: Choose a BusyBox Build
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me choose the correct BusyBox Windows build for my CPU and Windows version.

prompt_templates.title: Run a Unix Command
prompt_templates.prompt: Show me how to run a Unix-style command through busybox.exe on Windows.

prompt_templates.title: Use BusyBox from Another Directory
prompt_templates.prompt: Explain how to run busybox.exe when my current working directory is different.

prompt_templates.title: Review Installation Safety
prompt_templates.prompt: Review the BusyBox Windows setup steps and identify what I should verify before running the executable.

output_examples.input: I need Unix commands on a Windows machine.
output_examples.output: The skill guides you to use a Win32 BusyBox executable and prefix commands with busybox.exe.

output_examples.input: I am not sure which BusyBox file to download.
output_examples.output: The skill asks you to check processor and Windows version details before selecting a matching build.

output_examples.input: I want to run BusyBox outside its folder.
output_examples.output: The skill explains that you should use the absolute path to busybox.exe when working from another directory.

best_practices: Verify the source and integrity of any downloaded executable before running it.
Use this skill only on Windows systems where BusyBox is needed.
Review each PowerShell command before pasting it into a terminal.
anti_patterns: Do not run a downloaded busybox.exe without checking its source.
Do not use this workflow on Unix systems where native tools already exist.
Do not assume every BusyBox command behaves exactly like a full GNU tool.

faq.question: What does this skill help me do?
faq.answer: It helps you use a Windows BusyBox executable to run many Unix-style command line tools.

faq.question: Does the skill install BusyBox automatically?
faq.answer: No. It provides manual commands and guidance for selecting and using a BusyBox build.

faq.question: Can I use it on macOS or Linux?
faq.answer: No. The skill explicitly says to use it only on Windows.

faq.question: Why does it mention PowerShell and cmd.exe?
faq.answer: The setup commands are written for PowerShell, with guidance for running them from cmd.exe.

faq.question: Is there a security concern?
faq.answer: Yes. You should verify any downloaded executable before running it on your computer.

faq.question: Does it cover BusyBox command details?
faq.answer: It shows how to list available commands and links to external documentation for more detail.
